metadata.title: Why Some Missing Data Lies About Itself
metadata.purpose: vox-explainer slate cut of Computational Skepticism for AI candidate 24. Source: books/computational-skepticism-for-ai/chapters/05-data-validation-reconstructing-the-epistemic-frame-behind-a-dataset.md (MNAR missing data, lines 129-135, 275, 287).
metadata.color_semantics: TEAL = present / visible data; CRIMSON = missing / the gap; GOLD = the key insight / the direction of bias; SLATE = the imputed values / naive fix
metadata.note: EXCLUSIONS: no MCAR/MAR formal definitions, no imputation-method catalog, no missingno tooling, no join-failure case

beats.narration_text: Some missing data lies about itself.

card.copy:
Why Some Missing Data
Lies About Itself

beats.narration_text: You're analyzing income data. Eighteen percent of respondents didn't fill in their income. You compute the average of what's visible and impute the missing values. The result: a dataset whose income distribution is systematically too low — because the people who didn't report are the high earners, and the high earners were the ones most likely not to report.
If you can only see the data that's present — and the missing data is missing precisely because of what it contains — how do you detect a bias you can't see from inside the data?

props.headline: If you can only see the data that's present — and…

card.copy:
If the missing data is missing because
of what it contains — how do you detect it?

beats.narration_text: There are three ways data can be missing. Missing completely at random — a sensor failure, no pattern. Missing at random — missingness correlates with something visible, like age. And missing not at random: the value itself determines whether it shows up. High earners don't report. Sick patients leave before the last measurement.

The problem with missing-not-at-random is that it looks exactly like the other kinds from inside the dataset. The holes appear. You don't know why they're there. And the reason — the value itself — is exactly what's absent.
The bias is exactly in the direction you cannot measure.

props.headline: The bias is exactly in the direction you cannot measure.

beats.image_description: A spreadsheet with a column of income figures, where many cells in the upper range are blank or greyed out — missing values concentrated at the top of the distribution

beats.narration_text: When you impute — replace the missing values with the visible mean or median — you fill the high-income gaps with low-income numbers. The distribution you see is skewed low. You impute from it. The resulting dataset is systematically wrong, in exactly the direction you can't see from inside. The more data you have, the more confidently wrong your imputation becomes.
Missing not at random: the probability of being missing depends on the value itself. Standard imputation produces biased estimates, and the bias is exactly in the direction you cannot measure.

Standard exploratory analysis does not detect missing-not-at-random. The pattern only becomes visible by reasoning about who would not report this value and why. You cannot measure the bias from inside the data. You have to think outside it.
You detect missing-not-at-random by thinking carefully about who would not report this value and why — not by looking at the data alone.

For every column with significant missing values: ask the question standard tools skip. Not how many are missing. Why are they missing? If the answer is — they're missing because of what they contain — imputing from what you can see will bake the bias in permanently, in precisely the direction you cannot measure.

A clinical trial tests a new pain medication. Two hundred patients start. Forty-three drop out before the final measurement — nearly all of them reporting severe side effects in week two. The final dataset shows a strong treatment effect. The researchers impute missing outcomes from the completers. The result is systematically too optimistic. The patients who would have dragged the average down aren't there. They left because of what they experienced.

When data is missing because of its own value, the visible distribution silently lies. Imputing from what you can see bakes the bias in. Detect it not by looking at the data — but by reasoning about who would not be there.

props.headline: When data is missing because of its own value, the visible…

card.copy:
Missing because of its own value.
Bias in the direction you can't measure.
card.sub: Detect it by reasoning about who's absent — not by analyzing what's present.

beats.name: OutroSeries
beats.narration_text: Part of the Computational Skepticism for AI series.
